CrowdStrike Falcon — SOC L1 SOP

Harris Health System — Security Operations Center v1.0 — Mar 2026 Sections 🏠 Overview 🖥️ Console Navigation 🔔 Reading a Detection 🌳 Process Tree Investigation 🔒 Host Containment & Lift 🎯 IOC Management 🔍 Hash & IOC Lookup 💻 Real-Time Response 🚫 Common False Positives Cheat Sheets 📖 Detection Names Decoder ⚔️ MITRE ATT&CK Decoder 🚦 Severity → SLA Mapping ⌨️ Keyboard Shortcuts CrowdStrike Falcon — SOC L1 Standard Operating Procedure This is the complete L1 analyst guide for CrowdStrike Falcon, Harris Health's primary EDR platform . It covers ~13,000 IT endpoints across 2 hospitals and 32 clinics. Microsoft Defender for Endpoint runs in passive mode alongside Falcon (for Purview telemetry only). CrowdStrike Overwatch provides 24/7 human-led threat hunting on top of automated detections. Console Login: falcon.crowdstrike.com (https://falcon.crowdstrike.com) — Use your Harris Health SSO credentials. If locked out, contact your SOC Manager. What Harris Health Uses CrowdStrike Falcon For
ModulePurpose at Harris HealthStatus
Falcon PreventNext-gen antivirus — blocks known malware, exploits, and ransomware at the endpointActive
Falcon Insight XDREndpoint detection and response — behavioral analytics, process tree visibility, IOC matchingActive
Falcon Overwatch24/7 human-led threat hunting — proactive alerts from CrowdStrike's elite threat huntersActive (Phase 0)
Falcon Intelligence (CAO)Threat intel, dark web monitoring for Harris Health, adversary tracking, healthcare threat profilesActive (Phase 0)
IOC ManagementCustom indicator blocking/detection — IPs, domains, file hashes pushed from TIP or manuallyActive
Real-Time Response (RTR)Remote shell to endpoints for live forensics, file retrieval, process kill, script executionActive
Network ContainmentIsolate a compromised endpoint from the network while maintaining CrowdStrike cloud connectivityActive
Key Architecture Note: CrowdStrike Falcon is the primary EDR. Microsoft Defender for Endpoint runs in passive mode alongside it (for Purview DLP telemetry only, not for active protection). If you see a Defender alert, it's informational — CrowdStrike is the blocking/containment tool. A 6-9 month parallel evaluation is underway to make a data-driven decision on whether to keep both. 🖥️ Console Navigation Use this section to find your way around the Falcon console. The console is accessed via falcon.crowdstrike.com (https://falcon.crowdstrike.com) using Harris Health SSO. Key Areas You'll Use Daily 1 Activity Dashboard (Home) Where: Landing page after login, or left menu → Activity → Detections What it shows: All active detections across the environment, sorted by severity and time. This is your primary alert queue for CrowdStrike alerts. L1 Action: Start here every shift. Filter by severity (Critical/High first), then review unassigned detections. 2 Host Management Where: Left menu → Host setup and management → Host Management What it shows: All enrolled endpoints — hostname, IP, OS, sensor version, containment status, last seen time. L1 Action: Search for a specific host by hostname or IP. Check if a host is online, contained, or in Reduced Functionality Mode (RFM). This is where you initiate Network Containment . 3 Threat Intelligence → IOC Management Where: Left menu → Threat Intelligence → IOC Management What it shows: All custom IOCs (IPs, domains, file hashes) that have been added — either from the TIP automatically or manually by analysts. L1 Action: Add new IOCs from threat advisories, search for existing IOCs, check if an indicator is already blocked. 4 Threat Intelligence → Intelligence Where: Left menu → Threat Intelligence → Intelligence What it shows: CrowdStrike's threat intelligence database — adversary profiles, malware families, vulnerability intelligence, IOC reputation lookups.

Recurring FP? If you see the same false positive pattern 3+ times, submit a detection tuning request to your SOC Manager. They'll work with CrowdStrike to create an exclusion or adjust the detection rule so it stops firing on benign activity. 📖 Detection Names Decoder Common CrowdStrike detection names and what they mean. When you see a detection fire, use this table to quickly understand the category.
Detection NameWhat It MeansTypical Action
Malware DetectionStatic analysis (ML or signature) identified the file as malwareCheck if blocked. If blocked → verify quarantine, close as TP-Prevented. If not blocked → isolate host.
Intel DetectionA file hash, IP, or domain matched a CrowdStrike Intelligence IOC or a custom IOC you addedHigh confidence. Check which IOC matched. If custom IOC from your TIP → verify the source advisory.
Suspicious ProcessBehavioral analysis flagged a process doing something unusual (but not confirmed malware)Investigate the process tree. Check command-line args. May be FP (IT tool) or TP (attacker).
Machine Learning DetectionCrowdStrike's ML model flagged the file based on characteristics, not a known signatureHigher FP rate than Intel Detection. Check file path, signer, and VirusTotal. If unsigned + suspicious path → likely TP.
Overwatch DetectionA CrowdStrike human threat hunter identified this activityAUTO-ESCALATE to L2 — highest confidence. Do not close without L2 review.
Prevention / BlockedFalcon Prevent blocked execution of a file or processVerify the block in the detection detail. If a known-bad file was blocked → TP-Prevented, close with notes.
Ransomware DetectionBehavioral analysis detected ransomware-like activity (mass file encryption, extension changes)IMMEDIATE ACTION — contain host, escalate to L2 + SOC Manager + CISO. See Ransomware playbook.
Credential TheftProcess accessed LSASS memory or performed credential dumping techniquesCRITICAL — indicates mimikatz or similar. Contain host, escalate to L2.
IOA (Indicator of Attack)Behavioral pattern matched a known attack technique — even if no malware file is presentInvestigate the full process tree. IOAs catch fileless attacks and LOLBin abuse. Context is everything.
⚔️ MITRE ATT&CK Quick Decoder CrowdStrike maps every detection to a MITRE ATT&CK tactic and technique. The tactic tells you what the attacker is trying to do in the kill chain. Use this to assess how far an attack has progressed. Kill Chain Rule of Thumb: The further down this table you go, the deeper the attacker is in the environment and the more urgently you need to respond. Early-stage tactics (Reconnaissance, Initial Access) may be probing. Late-stage tactics (Lateral Movement, Exfiltration, Impact) mean the attacker has progressed significantly — escalate immediately.

## 🎯 IOC Management — Deep Dive

**Summary:** IOC Management in CrowdStrike Falcon is where you add, search, and manage custom Indicators of Compromise to either **detect** or **block** known-malicious file hashes, IPs, and domains across Harris Health's ~13,000 endpoints.

---

### 📍 Where to Find It

**Navigation:** Left menu → Threat Intelligence → IOC Management

This shows all custom IOCs (IPs, domains, file hashes) that have been added — either from the **TIP (Sentinel) automatically** or **manually by analysts**.[1]

---

### ➕ How to Add a Custom IOC — Step by Step



**Step 1 — Navigate to IOC Management**
Go to: Left menu → Threat Intelligence → IOC Management. You'll see the list of all existing IOCs.

**Step 2 — Click "Add IOC"**
Select the IOC type:
- **File Hash** (SHA256 preferred, also SHA1 or MD5)
- **Domain**
- **IP Address**

**Step 3 — Enter the IOC value**
- For hashes → paste the full **SHA256**.
- For domains → enter **without protocol** (e.g., `evil-domain.com`, NOT `https://evil-domain.com`).
- For IPs → enter the **IPv4 address**.

**Step 4 — Set the Action**
- 🔍 **Detect** — alerts only, does **not block**. Use when you want to **monitor**.
- 🚫 **Detect & Prevent** — blocks execution/connection **AND** alerts. Use for **confirmed malicious IOCs** and active exploitation.

**Step 5 — Set Severity and Description**
Match the advisory severity (Critical, High, Medium, Low). In the description, include the source, e.g.:
- `"Health-ISAC Advisory 2026-042"`
- `"CISA KEV — CVE-2026-12345"`

**Step 6 — Set Expiration**
Harris Health policy: **no permanent IOCs**.
- Set **30 days** for actively exploited threats (renew if still active).
- Set **90 days** for general threat intel.
- IOCs auto-expire and are cleaned up.

**Step 7 — Save and Verify**
Click **Save**. Verify the IOC appears in the IOC Management list. Monitor the Detection Summary for any hits.

[2]

---

### 🔍 How to Search for Existing IOCs

Use the **search bar at the top of IOC Management**. Paste a hash, IP, or domain to check if it's already in the system **before adding a duplicate**.[2]

> ✅ **Always search before adding** — duplicate IOCs can cause confusion and clutter the list.

---

### 🔎 How IOC Hits Appear in Detections

When a file or connection matches one of your custom IOCs, it fires as an **"Intel Detection"** in the alert queue.

**Intel Detection** means: a file hash, IP, or domain matched a CrowdStrike Intelligence IOC or a custom IOC you added.

**Typical Action:** High confidence. Check which IOC matched. If the custom IOC came from your TIP → verify the source advisory.[3]

---

### 🔍 Hash & IOC Lookup Priority

When you're unsure if an IOC is malicious and need to investigate before adding it, follow this lookup priority:



**1️⃣ CrowdStrike Intelligence (first)**
- Where: Threat Intelligence → Intelligence → paste the hash, IP, or domain in the search bar.
- Why first: CrowdStrike's intel database includes **healthcare-specific threat data** and correlates with adversary campaigns. It's the most contextually relevant source for Harris Health.
- What to look for: Malware family, associated adversary group, kill chain phase, severity rating, and any related IOCs.

**2️⃣ VirusTotal (second)**
- Where: [virustotal.com](https://www.virustotal.com) → paste the hash, IP, or domain.
- Why second: VirusTotal aggregates results from **70+ antivirus engines**. Good for confirmation and broad coverage, but lacks the adversary context that CrowdStrike provides.
- What to look for: Detection ratio (e.g., 45/70 engines detect = confirmed malicious), community comments, and behavioral analysis results.

[2]

---

### ⚠️ Critical OPSEC Warning

**Do NOT upload actual files from Harris Health endpoints to VirusTotal.** VirusTotal is a public service — uploaded files become visible to other researchers and potentially adversaries. **Only submit hashes (SHA256) for lookup, never the actual file.** If you need to detonate a file in a sandbox, use CrowdStrike's sandbox or coordinate with L2.[2]

---

### 📋 Quick Reference Summary

| IOC Type | Format | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **File Hash** | SHA256 (preferred) | `4e106c973f28acfc...` |
| **Domain** | No protocol prefix | `evil-domain.com` |
| **IP Address** | IPv4 | `192.168.1.100` |

| Action | When to Use |
|---|---|
| **Detect** | Monitoring / unsure / threat intel feeds |
| **Detect & Prevent** | Confirmed malicious / active exploitation |

| Expiration | When to Use |
|---|---|
| **30 days** | Actively exploited threats |
| **90 days** | General threat intel |

---

💡 **Need help with a specific scenario?** For example — *"I have a hash from a threat advisory, how do I add it?"* or *"A detection fired matching an IOC, what do I do next?"* — just ask and I'll walk you through it step by step!
